audience: startup founders and CTOs building AI features
problem: Most AI features work perfectly in demos but fail silently in production under real load
persists: because teams optimize for model accuracy but never define concurrency limits, fallback behavior, or operational boundaries before shipping
principle: Before exposing any AI endpoint to users, measure token throughput, cap concurrent requests, define explicit timeout behavior, and keep a lighter fallback model ready. Building Cova (apps.apple.com/us/app/cova/id6748680152) taught me this the hard way — the DeepSeek transcription pipeline worked perfectly until three brokers recorded simultaneously.
cta: What broke first in your AI feature after going live — the model, the infrastructure, or the UX?

audience: founders and product owners preparing to launch
problem: The last 5% of a SaaS build consistently takes 50% of the total time
persists: because edge cases, environment config mismatches, and real user error states are invisible until actual users hit them in production
principle: Ship to one real user before optimizing anything. On WattVue (wattvue.com), the solar monitoring dashboard worked perfectly in staging. First real client exposed three edge cases in the data pipeline we had never considered. One real user beats 100 internal test scenarios.
cta: What nearly killed your last product launch that you never saw coming in testing?

audience: founders hiring developers and technical leads
problem: Founders hire developers who sound confident, not developers who are actually right
persists: because most developers explain their solution without explaining the tradeoff — so clients cannot tell the difference between a good decision and an overconfident one
principle: Explaining the tradeoff before the solution is what builds long-term client trust. On EverCare, I told the client the scheduling feature would take three weeks longer if we wanted it to be multi-tenant safe. They agreed. Six months later that decision saved them a complete rewrite.
cta: How do you explain technical tradeoffs to non-technical stakeholders without losing their confidence?

audience: CTOs and technical leads integrating LLMs into products
problem: LLM integration looks simple until you need streaming, context management, cost control, and fallback behavior all working together in production
persists: because most tutorials show the happy path API call — not the operational layer that makes it reliable at scale
principle: Route simple tasks to faster cheaper models. Reserve strong models for complex reasoning. Log every inference with latency, model used, and routing decision. On Cova, routing meeting transcription through DeepSeek and conversational queries through a lighter model cut costs by 60% without users noticing.
cta: Do you route AI tasks by cost, latency, or output quality first — and how do you measure the tradeoff?

audience: founders and developers building first SaaS products
problem: Most MVPs are over-architected in the wrong places and under-engineered in the places that actually matter
persists: because developers default to patterns from their last job rather than asking what this specific product needs to survive its first 100 users
principle: Start with one service, managed Postgres, and simple deploys. Add complexity only when metrics show you need it. The biggest mistake I see in early SaaS products is microservices before product-market fit. WattVue started as a monolith. It still is. It serves thousands of users across the US.
cta: What architectural decision from your MVP do you wish you had made differently?

audience: developers and CTOs managing production deployments
problem: Staging works. Production breaks. The gap is almost always environment configuration, not code.
persists: because teams treat environment variables, secrets, and runtime configuration as an afterthought rather than first-class concerns in the deployment pipeline
principle: Every environment must be explicitly defined, version-controlled, and verified before deploy. Pin runtime versions. Document every secret. Test migrations against a production-equivalent database before touching prod. I have seen more production incidents caused by a wrong DATABASE_URL than by bad code.
cta: What environment configuration issue caused your most painful production incident?

audience: founders hiring freelancers and freelancers looking for clients
problem: Most freelance projects fail not because of technical skill but because of scope, communication, and expectation mismatches in the first two weeks
persists: because both clients and developers avoid the uncomfortable conversation about what done actually means until it is too late to align
principle: Define done before writing a line of code. Every project I scope now has three columns: what is in, what is explicitly out, and what requires a separate conversation. This single practice has eliminated scope disputes across 26 Upwork projects with a 100% job success score.
cta: What is the one conversation you wish you had earlier on a project that went sideways?

audience: CTOs and tech leads managing growing React codebases
problem: React codebases become unmaintainable not because React is bad but because boundaries between UI, state, and data fetching are never enforced
persists: because teams ship features fast without establishing component contracts, and the technical debt accumulates invisibly until a new developer cannot understand the codebase in under a day
principle: Components do one thing. Custom hooks own all side effects. Server and client concerns never mix in the same file. On EverCare, refactoring an 800-line scheduling component into four focused units reduced bug reports in that module by more than half.
cta: What is the single architectural rule you enforce most strictly in your React codebase?

audience: founders and developers building on Supabase
problem: Supabase feels like magic until you hit multi-tenant data isolation, RLS policy gaps, and environment separation in production
persists: because the local development experience is so smooth that teams skip the hard questions about tenant isolation and secret management until a real client's data is at risk
principle: Separate Supabase projects for dev and production from day one. Version control every RLS policy. Test tenant isolation explicitly before onboarding any real user. I have audited three Supabase codebases where the RLS policies looked correct but had subtle bypass vectors through URL manipulation.
cta: What Supabase production issue caught you off guard that was not obvious during development?

audience: founders and developers implementing payment systems
problem: Payment integrations break user trust when teams only handle the happy path and ignore holds, capture timing, and webhook ordering
persists: because authorization feels like the finish line, but the real failures happen asynchronously in hold expiry, capture failure, and dispute state transitions that nobody mapped before shipping
principle: Model every payment state explicitly in Postgres. Make every Stripe webhook handler idempotent with dedupe keys. Build a reconciliation worker that runs every 15 minutes. The hard part is not capturing a payment — it is unwinding a hold precisely when the customer already saw a confirmation screen.
cta: Which payment edge case caused your biggest production incident — hold expiry, webhook ordering, or something else?

audience: founders building vertical AI products for specific industries
problem: Vertical AI assistants fail in production when teams optimize for demo quality instead of workflow reliability for real users under time pressure
persists: because a smooth chat interface is not the same as a tool that works for a broker in a client meeting, a field technician with spotty signal, or a doctor between appointments
principle: Separate conversation UX from task execution. Stream transcription asynchronously. Index key moments for retrieval. Expose follow-up actions as explicit workflows not free-form prompts. Cova (apps.apple.com/us/app/cova/id6748680152) went through four architecture iterations before insurance brokers could use it reliably in live client meetings.
cta: When you build vertical AI tools, what fails first for real users — retrieval quality, workflow clarity, or mobile reliability?

audience: CTOs and senior engineers designing multi-service systems
problem: Architecture becomes harder to change when service boundaries are drawn around team structure instead of around data ownership and failure modes
persists: because a clean architecture diagram can still hide chatty APIs, duplicated state, and unclear ownership that only shows up under production load or when the team grows
principle: Draw boundaries around the write path and blast radius first. Communication method — queues, webhooks, synchronous APIs — is a secondary decision based on latency and recovery needs, not diagram aesthetics. Define who owns the canonical state for each entity before writing a single service.
cta: What service boundary do you wish you had defined differently before your system grew?

audience: CTOs and engineers shipping AI features to real users
problem: Model endpoints that pass every test still collapse in production because concurrency, queuing, and timeout behavior were never measured before going live
persists: because health checks report up while the GPU memory fills and requests queue silently — the failure mode is invisible until users see errors
principle: Measure token throughput before launch. Set explicit concurrency limits. Define timeout behavior and structured error responses. Deploy a smaller fallback model on a separate path. A week of upfront profiling prevents the kind of production incident that destroys user trust in an AI feature permanently.
cta: What was the first real bottleneck you hit serving a model to actual users?

audience: technical leads and founders managing development velocity
problem: Backend development stalls while waiting for a frozen scope document even when the architecture path is already clear
persists: because teams confuse documentation completeness with decision readiness — the API contracts, schema direction, and deploy target are often knowable well before the scope doc is finished
principle: Define narrow API contracts early. Deploy a thin service. Run migrations in CI against real infrastructure. Promote the same artifact through environments. Ship the knowable parts while the unknowable parts are still being decided. Waiting for perfect scope is how projects lose weeks.
cta: When scope lags, do you ship the thin service first, define contracts first, or wait — and why?

audience: engineering teams managing production deployments
problem: Production surprises keep appearing because CI pipelines verify code compilation but not deployment behavior
persists: because tests run with different environment variables than production, migrations execute in a different order than expected, and nobody treats the pipeline as the source of truth for a releasable artifact
principle: Build one immutable image per commit. Run migrations as an explicit controlled step. Gate deploys behind smoke tests. Wire CloudWatch alarms to the same latency and error metrics you watch after every rollout. A green pipeline should mean deployable, not just buildable.
cta: What is the one check you refuse to skip before merging to main?

audience: founders and CTOs hiring their first AI engineer
problem: Most people who call themselves AI engineers have only ever wired up an API call and have never operated a model in production
persists: because a working demo is easy to produce and impossible to distinguish from real depth in a 30-minute interview unless you know which questions expose the gap
principle: Ask how they handle the unhappy path, not the happy one. A real AI engineer talks first about timeouts, fallback models, cost per request, evaluation, and what happens when the model returns garbage. Someone who only describes the prompt and the response has built a demo, not a product. Hire for operational judgment, not prompt cleverness.
cta: If you have hired AI talent, what question separated the real engineers from the API wrappers?

audience: founders funding or scoping AI features
problem: The distance between an impressive AI demo and a reliable AI product is where most AI budgets quietly disappear
persists: because the demo is 10% of the work but 90% of the excitement, so teams approve the project based on the part that was already easy
principle: Treat the demo as the start of discovery, not proof of delivery. The real work is everything the demo skipped: concurrency, latency under load, cost ceilings, evaluation against real data, graceful failure, and a human fallback. Budget for the 90% nobody clapped for, or the feature never ships past the pitch.
cta: What surprised you most about the cost of taking an AI feature from demo to production?

audience: non-technical founders making their first engineering hire
problem: The first technical hire decides whether your product is buildable for the next two years or rewritten in six months
persists: because founders optimize for who can ship the MVP fastest instead of who makes decisions that still hold up after the third pivot
principle: Hire someone who asks what you are trying to learn, not someone who immediately lists technologies. The best early engineers reduce scope, defer complexity, and tell you what not to build. Speed without judgment just gets you to the wrong place faster. The first hire sets the ceiling for everyone who comes after.
cta: Founders: what did you get right or wrong about your very first technical hire?

audience: CTOs and founders running AI features in production
problem: The real cost of an AI feature is not building it, it is operating it month after month at scale
persists: because pricing looks trivial on a single test call, then quietly multiplies across every user, retry, and oversized context window once real traffic arrives
principle: Decide your cost ceiling before you ship, not after the bill arrives. Route cheap tasks to cheaper models, cache aggressively, trim context to what the model actually needs, and log cost per request so a spike is visible the same day. AI economics are an operations problem, not a model-selection problem.
cta: What unexpectedly drove up your AI bill once real users showed up?

audience: founders and tech leads managing delivery velocity
problem: Teams lose more time polishing things no user asked for than they ever lose to bugs
persists: because polishing feels productive and safe, while putting an unfinished thing in front of a real user feels risky even though that is where the actual learning is
principle: Ship the smallest version that a real user can react to, then let their reaction set the next priority. Perfect internal quality on a feature nobody needed is just expensive procrastination. The goal is not to avoid mistakes, it is to make them cheaply and early where they still cost almost nothing to fix.
cta: What is something you over-polished that no user ever noticed or cared about?

audience: non-technical founders managing developers and agencies
problem: Non-technical founders cannot tell whether the work they are paying for is solid or quietly accumulating debt that will surface later
persists: because progress is reported in features shipped, while the things that decide long-term cost — tests, error handling, clear ownership — are invisible unless you know to ask
principle: You do not need to read code to evaluate engineering. Ask what happens when this fails, how we would know, and how hard the next change will be. A developer who answers those clearly is protecting your future. One who waves them away is selling you speed today and a rewrite tomorrow.
cta: Non-technical founders: how do you judge whether your dev work is actually solid?

audience: founders and CTOs building data-heavy or document-heavy products
problem: Features that process large batches feel fine in testing, then time out and fail the moment a real user uploads real volume
persists: because the work runs synchronously inside the request, so the first big job blocks everything and the user just sees a spinner that never resolves
principle: Move heavy work off the request path and into a queue. On an AI ATS and CV analyzer I built, scoring resumes one-by-one in the request would have been unusable, so a Celery and Redis pipeline scores 1,000 resumes in under 10 minutes while the user gets progress instead of a timeout. Anything slow or bursty belongs in a background worker, not the HTTP handler.
cta: What was the first feature you had to move from synchronous to background processing, and what forced it?

audience: founders building voice or real-time AI products
problem: Voice AI that feels magical in a recording feels broken the moment there is a one-second delay between a person speaking and the system responding
persists: because each stage — speech to text, model reasoning, text to speech — adds latency, and stacking them naively turns a snappy demo into an awkward conversation nobody wants to have
principle: Treat latency as a first-class feature, not an afterthought. On Jarvis, a voice-first assistant I built, hitting sub-300ms response meant streaming each stage instead of waiting for it to finish: local speech-to-text, a tight reasoning loop, and text-to-speech that starts speaking before the full answer is ready. In real-time products, perceived speed matters more than raw model quality.
cta: For anyone building voice or real-time AI, where does your latency actually go — transcription, the model, or playback?

audience: founders and operators considering agentic AI tools for real workflows
problem: Agentic AI tools demo beautifully and then fail silently in production, doing the wrong thing without anyone noticing until the damage is done
persists: because teams trust the agent to be right instead of building the validation and alerting that catches it when it is wrong
principle: An agent is only as trustworthy as the guardrails around it. On an agentic tool I built that fetches jobs and drafts tailored proposals every 20 minutes, the part that made it usable was not the model, it was the validation layer: lint rules, auto-repair, and failure alerts so there were zero silent failures in production. Build the agent to fail loudly, not to be perfect.
cta: Would you trust an AI agent to act in your business automatically, and what guardrail would you require first?
